coverage_notes: Covers writing formulas from ion charges, naming ionic compounds (including transition metals with Roman numerals), naming covalent compounds (prefixes), and common polyatomic ions. Essential literacy for all subsequent chemistry.

rendering_guide.section: Headed paragraph block with spacing above/below
rendering_guide.callout: Highlighted box. Styles: definition, key_idea, fun_fact, remember, mind_blow
rendering_guide.list: Styled list. Styles: icon_list, two_column, comparison, challenge, numbered_steps
rendering_guide.transition: Italic bridge text, lighter font weight

student_flow.step_1: READ Part 1 (hook вЂ” chemical formulas are a language)
student_flow.step_2: READ Part 2 (writing ionic formulas from charges)
student_flow.step_4: READ Part 3 (naming ionic and covalent compounds)
student_flow.step_6: READ Part 4 (polyatomic ions вЂ” grouped atoms with charges)
student_flow.step_8: READ Part 5 (big picture + bridge to chemical_reactions)

parts.purpose: Hook вЂ” formulas and names are chemistry's alphabet and vocabulary

blocks.body: When a chemist writes Hв‚‚O, every other chemist on Earth knows exactly what it means вЂ” two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom. No ambiguity. No translation needed. Chemical formulas are a universal language, and learning to read and write them is like learning the alphabet of chemistry.

Chemical formulas tell you WHAT atoms are in a compound and HOW MANY of each. Chemical names tell you what the compound is in words. Both follow strict rules so that every compound has exactly one correct formula and one correct name.

The good news: the rules are systematic. Once you learn the patterns for ionic and covalent naming, you can name any compound you encounter and write any formula from a name. No memorization of individual compounds needed вЂ” just patterns.

blocks.title: You Already Know Some
blocks.body: Hв‚‚O = water. COв‚‚ = carbon dioxide. NaCl = sodium chloride (table salt). Oв‚‚ = oxygen gas. You've been reading chemical formulas without realizing it. Now you'll learn the system behind them.

Reading a formula: the element symbol tells you WHICH element. The subscript (small number) tells you HOW MANY. Hв‚‚O: 2 hydrogens, 1 oxygen (no subscript = 1). Cв‚†Hв‚Ѓв‚‚Oв‚† (glucose): 6 carbons, 12 hydrogens, 6 oxygens.

Notice: the subscripts in ionic formulas always produce a neutral compound (total positive = total negative). The subscripts in covalent formulas reflect how many atoms each central atom bonds to. Both follow from valence electron counts. The formulas aren't arbitrary вЂ” they're electron math.

Chemical formulas are like addresses for compounds. Hв‚‚O doesn't live at 'two hydrogens, one oxygen' by coincidence вЂ” that IS its identity. Every compound has a unique formula, just like every house has a unique address.

The naming system has been standardized internationally since 1919 by IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry). It's like the postal service of chemistry вЂ” agreed-upon rules so a chemist in Tokyo and a chemist in Brazil can read each other's work without confusion.
There are two naming systems: one for ionic compounds (metal + nonmetal) and one for covalent compounds (nonmetal + nonmetal). Different rules, different vibes. Let's learn both.

parts.purpose: Core teaching вЂ” using ion charges to write correct ionic formulas

blocks.title: The Charge-Balance Rule
blocks.body: Every ionic compound is electrically neutral вЂ” total positive charge equals total negative charge. To write a formula, you balance the charges of the cation and anion.

items.label: Step 1: Write the ion charges
items.body: Identify the cation charge (metal) and anion charge (nonmetal). Use the periodic table: Group 1 = +1, Group 2 = +2, Group 13 = +3. Group 15 = в€’3, Group 16 = в€’2, Group 17 = в€’1.

items.label: Step 2: Criss-cross the numbers
items.body: Take the NUMBER from each charge and make it the SUBSCRIPT of the OTHER ion. CaВІвЃє and ClвЃ» в†’ the 2 from calcium becomes chlorine's subscript, the 1 from chlorine becomes calcium's subscript в†’ CaClв‚‚.

items.label: Step 3: Simplify if needed
items.body: If both subscripts share a common factor, reduce. MgВІвЃє and OВІвЃ» в†’ criss-cross gives Mgв‚‚Oв‚‚ в†’ simplify to MgO.

blocks.title: Practice Examples
blocks.body: Let's work through several to build the pattern.

items.definition: Criss-cross: Caв‚ѓ(POв‚„)в‚‚ вЂ” parentheses around polyatomic ion

blocks.title: Parentheses Rule
blocks.body: When you need MORE THAN ONE polyatomic ion, put it in parentheses with the subscript outside. Caв‚ѓ(POв‚„)в‚‚ means 3 calcium ions and 2 phosphate groups. Never break apart a polyatomic ion.

blocks.title: Verify: Charges Must Cancel
blocks.body: Always check: total positive = total negative. Alв‚‚Oв‚ѓ: (2 Г— +3) = +6. (3 Г— в€’2) = в€’6. Net charge = 0. вњ“ If they don't cancel, the formula is wrong.

blocks.title: Work These Out With Paper
blocks.body: Write the ion charges, criss-cross, simplify, then verify the charges cancel.

items.label: Potassium + Bromine в†’ ?
items.hints: KвЃє + BrвЃ» в†’ criss-cross в†’ KBr. Check: +1 + (в€’1) = 0 вњ“

items.label: Barium + Chlorine в†’ ?
items.hints: BaВІвЃє + ClвЃ» в†’ criss-cross в†’ BaClв‚‚. Check: +2 + 2(в€’1) = 0 вњ“

items.label: Aluminum + Sulfur в†’ ?
items.hints: AlВівЃє + SВІвЃ» в†’ criss-cross в†’ Alв‚‚Sв‚ѓ. Check: 2(+3) + 3(в€’2) = +6 в€’ 6 = 0 вњ“

items.label: Sodium + Sulfate в†’ ?
items.hints: NaвЃє + SOв‚„ВІвЃ» в†’ criss-cross в†’ Naв‚‚SOв‚„. Check: 2(+1) + (в€’2) = 0 вњ“

blocks.body: The criss-cross method works because it automatically balances charges. The charge magnitude of one ion becomes the subscript of the other вЂ” guaranteeing equal total charge on both sides. It's mathematical elegance disguised as a simple trick.

blocks.title: The Criss-Cross Trick
blocks.body: Writing ionic formulas has a cheat code: the criss-cross method. Take the charge number from each ion and swap them as subscripts. That's it. It works every time.

AlВівЃє + OВІвЃ». Take the 3 from aluminum в†’ give it to oxygen. Take the 2 from oxygen в†’ give it to aluminum. Alв‚‚Oв‚ѓ. Done. Rust. You just wrote the formula for rust using a trick a 5-year-old could learn.

The criss-cross works because of basic algebra. If you need the total positive to equal total negative: (subscript_a Г— charge_a) + (subscript_b Г— charge_b) = 0. Setting each subscript to the OTHER ion's charge magnitude automatically satisfies this equation. It's not a trick вЂ” it's math wearing a disguise.

Parentheses rule: if you need more than one polyatomic ion, wrap it in parentheses. Caв‚ѓ(POв‚„)в‚‚ вЂ” the в‚‚ means 'two whole phosphate groups.' Writing Caв‚ѓPOв‚„в‚‚ would be wrong and confusing. Parentheses are the difference between 'two phosphate groups' and 'four oxygen atoms and two... what?' Punctuation matters in chemistry too.

questions.question: Write the formula for the ionic compound formed by calcium (CaВІвЃє) and nitrogen (NВівЃ»).
questions.explanation: Criss-cross: the 3 from NВівЃ» becomes Ca's subscript, the 2 from CaВІвЃє becomes N's subscript в†’ Caв‚ѓNв‚‚. Verify: 3(+2) + 2(в€’3) = +6 в€’ 6 = 0 вњ“

questions.question: Iron(III) and oxygen form Feв‚‚Oв‚ѓ. How do you verify this formula is correct?

options.A: Count total atoms: 2 + 3 = 5, which is odd, so it's correct
options.B: Check that total positive charge equals total negative charge: 2(+3) = +6, 3(в€’2) = в€’6, net = 0
options.C: Fe always has a subscript of 2 in any compound
options.D: The formula matches the periodic table row numbers

questions.explanation: Every ionic formula must be electrically neutral. Feв‚‚Oв‚ѓ: 2 iron(III) ions = 2 Г— (+3) = +6. 3 oxide ions = 3 Г— (в€’2) = в€’6. Total: +6 + (в€’6) = 0. Charges balance. Formula confirmed.

parts.purpose: Teach naming conventions for both ionic and covalent compounds

blocks.title: Two Systems, Two Compound Types
blocks.body: Ionic and covalent compounds have different naming rules. The first thing to determine is: is it ionic (metal + nonmetal) or covalent (nonmetal + nonmetal)?

blocks.title: Naming Ionic Compounds
blocks.body: Rule: [metal name] + [nonmetal root + '-ide']. NaCl = sodium chloride. MgO = magnesium oxide. CaFв‚‚ = calcium fluoride. The metal name stays as-is. The nonmetal drops its ending and adds -ide.

blocks.title: Transition Metals: The Roman Numeral System
blocks.body: Transition metals can form multiple charge states. Iron can be FeВІвЃє or FeВівЃє. Copper can be CuвЃє or CuВІвЃє. To specify which, use Roman numerals in parentheses after the metal name.

items.definition: Iron(II) chloride вЂ” Fe is +2 (since 2 ClвЃ» need +2 to balance)
Iron(III) chloride вЂ” Fe is +3 (since 3 ClвЃ» need +3)
Copper(II) oxide вЂ” Cu is +2 (OВІвЃ» needs +2)
Copper(I) oxide вЂ” each Cu is +1 (2 Г— +1 balances OВІвЃ»)

blocks.title: Naming Covalent Compounds
blocks.body: Rule: [prefix + first element] + [prefix + second element root + '-ide']. Use Greek prefixes: mono- (1), di- (2), tri- (3), tetra- (4), penta- (5), hexa- (6). Exception: never use 'mono-' on the FIRST element.

blocks.title: Ionic vs Covalent Naming at a Glance
blocks.body: Ionic: NO prefixes. Use Roman numerals for variable-charge metals. Covalent: USE prefixes (di-, tri-, tetra-...) to indicate atom counts. No Roman numerals. First step: identify the compound type from the elements involved.

blocks.title: Name These (Cover Answers First)
blocks.body: For each formula, decide: ionic or covalent? Then apply the correct naming rules.

items.label: MgBrв‚‚
items.hints: Mg (metal) + Br (nonmetal) = ionic. Mg is always +2 (Group 2, no Roman numeral needed). Answer: magnesium bromide.

items.label: Nв‚‚Oв‚„
items.hints: N + O = both nonmetals = covalent. Use prefixes. Answer: dinitrogen tetroxide.

items.label: CuClв‚‚
items.hints: Cu (metal) + Cl (nonmetal) = ionic. Cu is a transition metal вЂ” figure out its charge. 2 ClвЃ» = в€’2, so Cu = +2. Answer: copper(II) chloride.

items.label: CClв‚„
items.hints: C + Cl = both nonmetals = covalent. Answer: carbon tetrachloride.

blocks.body: The naming system is algorithmic: identify compound type в†’ apply the correct rule set в†’ generate the name. It works in reverse too: from a name, you can always write the formula. Name в†” formula is a two-way street.

blocks.title: Two Rule Books
blocks.body: Chemistry couldn't just have ONE naming system. No. It has two. Because ionic and covalent compounds are different enough to need different rules. But at least the rules are consistent.

items.label: Ionic Naming: Formal and Direct
items.body: Metal name + nonmetal-ide. NaCl = sodium chloride. CaO = calcium oxide. Elegant. Simple. No prefixes needed because charges dictate the ratio. If the metal has multiple possible charges (looking at you, iron), slap a Roman numeral on it: iron(III) chloride.
items.examples: MgClв‚‚ = magnesium chloride, FeClв‚ѓ = iron(III) chloride

items.label: Covalent Naming: Prefix Party
items.body: Greek prefixes everywhere. Mono, di, tri, tetra, penta, hexa. COв‚‚ = carbon dioxide. Nв‚‚Oв‚… = dinitrogen pentoxide. You need prefixes because two nonmetals can combine in MANY ratios (CO and COв‚‚ are very different). The prefix IS the number.
items.examples: SFв‚† = sulfur hexafluoride, PClв‚… = phosphorus pentachloride

blocks.body: The prefix 'mono-' is dropped from the FIRST element because it sounds awkward. 'Monocarbon dioxide' sounds ridiculous. 'Carbon dioxide' sounds clean. But you DO keep 'mono-' on the second element: carbon MONOxide (CO). English aesthetics trumping logic since 1919.

Pro tip for Roman numerals: the Roman numeral IS the charge. Iron(III) = FeВівЃє. Copper(II) = CuВІвЃє. The numeral isn't the subscript вЂ” it's the CHARGE. Then you use the charge to figure out the formula. Iron(III) chloride в†’ FeВівЃє + ClвЃ» в†’ criss-cross в†’ FeClв‚ѓ.

questions.question: What is the correct name for CuO, given that Cu is a transition metal?

options.C: Copper(II) oxide вЂ” Cu must be +2 to balance OВІвЃ»

questions.explanation: Cu is a transition metal (variable charges), so it needs a Roman numeral. OВІвЃ» has charge в€’2. For CuO to be neutral: Cu must be +2. Name: copper(II) oxide. 'Copper oxide' without a numeral is incomplete; 'copper dioxide' uses covalent prefixes, wrong for ionic.

questions.question: Nв‚‚Oв‚ѓ is a covalent compound. What is its correct name?
questions.explanation: Covalent naming uses prefixes for both elements: Nв‚‚ = dinitrogen, Oв‚ѓ = trioxide. Answer: dinitrogen trioxide. Roman numerals (option D) are for ionic compounds only. 'Nitrogen trioxide' (option A) is missing the 'di-' prefix for the two nitrogens.

parts.part_title: Polyatomic Ions вЂ” Teams of Atoms With a Charge
parts.purpose: Teach polyatomic ions as bonded groups that carry a charge and act as a unit

blocks.title: Ions Can Be Groups
blocks.body: So far, our ions have been single atoms: NaвЃє, ClвЃ», MgВІвЃє. But many ions are GROUPS of covalently bonded atoms that carry a net charge. These are polyatomic ions вЂ” 'many-atom ions.'

blocks.title: Polyatomic Ion
blocks.body: A group of two or more atoms covalently bonded together that carries a net electrical charge. The group acts as a single unit in ionic compounds. Example: SOв‚„ВІвЃ» (sulfate) is one sulfur bonded to four oxygens, with an overall в€’2 charge.

blocks.title: The Must-Know Polyatomic Ions
blocks.body: You'll encounter these repeatedly throughout chemistry. Learning the most common ones now saves enormous time later.

items.definition: Hydroxide вЂ” in every base (NaOH, KOH)
Carbonate вЂ” in limestone, baking soda base
Phosphate вЂ” in bones, DNA backbone
Ammonium вЂ” the only common POSITIVE polyatomic ion
Chlorate вЂ” in fireworks and matches

blocks.title: Writing Formulas With Polyatomic Ions
blocks.body: Treat the polyatomic ion as a single unit. Use the criss-cross method as before, but if you need more than one polyatomic ion, wrap it in parentheses.

items.definition: NHв‚„Cl (ammonium is the cation this time)

blocks.title: Naming With Polyatomic Ions
blocks.body: Polyatomic ions keep their own names вЂ” don't modify them. NaOH = sodium hydroxide (not 'sodium oxygen hydride'). Ca(NOв‚ѓ)в‚‚ = calcium nitrate. Alв‚‚(SOв‚„)в‚ѓ = aluminum sulfate. Just: [cation name] + [polyatomic ion name].

blocks.title: Spot Polyatomic Ions in Products
blocks.body: Check the ingredients on products around you. You'll find polyatomic ions everywhere.

items.label: Baking soda
items.hints: NaHCOв‚ѓ вЂ” sodium bicarbonate. Contains NaвЃє and HCOв‚ѓвЃ» (bicarbonate ion).

items.label: Tums/antacids
items.hints: CaCOв‚ѓ вЂ” calcium carbonate. Contains CaВІвЃє and COв‚ѓВІвЃ» (carbonate ion).

items.label: Fertilizer
items.hints: Often contains KNOв‚ѓ (potassium nitrate) or (NHв‚„)в‚‚SOв‚„ (ammonium sulfate). Packed with polyatomic ions.

items.label: Drain cleaner
items.hints: Often NaOH вЂ” sodium hydroxide (lye). Contains NaвЃє and OHвЃ» (hydroxide ion).

blocks.body: Polyatomic ions are hybrid entities вЂ” internally covalent (atoms sharing electrons), but externally ionic (the whole group has a charge and participates in ionic compounds). They bridge both bonding worlds.

blocks.title: Ion Teams
blocks.body: You know how individual players form teams? Polyatomic ions are exactly that. A group of atoms that bonded together covalently, picked up a charge, and now operate as a single unit in ionic compounds.

items.label: SOв‚„ВІвЃ» (Sulfate): Team 'Strong Acid Vibes'
items.body: 1 sulfur + 4 oxygens, overall charge в€’2. Shows up in Epsom salts, battery acid (Hв‚‚SOв‚„), and gypsum. One of the most common polyatomic ions in existence.

items.label: NHв‚„вЃє (Ammonium): The Rebel
items.body: The ONLY common positive polyatomic ion. 1 nitrogen + 4 hydrogens, charge +1. Acts like a metal ion despite containing no metal. Chemistry's undercover agent.

items.label: OHвЃ» (Hydroxide): Team 'Base'
items.body: 1 oxygen + 1 hydrogen, charge в€’1. Present in every base. NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)в‚‚. If it ends in hydroxide, it's a base. No exceptions.

blocks.body: The parentheses rule exists to prevent chaos. Ca(OH)в‚‚ means 2 hydroxide groups (each OHвЃ» intact). Without parentheses, CaOHв‚‚ would imply 1 oxygen and 2 hydrogens, which is meaningless. Parentheses in chemistry formulas aren't optional вЂ” they're structural. Treat them like seatbelts: always use them when needed.

questions.question: Calcium (CaВІвЃє) bonds with phosphate (POв‚„ВівЃ»). Write the correct formula.
questions.explanation: Criss-cross: the 3 from POв‚„ВівЃ» becomes calcium's subscript (Caв‚ѓ), the 2 from CaВІвЃє becomes phosphate's subscript (POв‚„)в‚‚. Parentheses required since we need 2 phosphate groups. Verify: 3(+2) + 2(в€’3) = +6 в€’ 6 = 0 вњ“

questions.question: What is the name of Alв‚‚(SOв‚„)в‚ѓ?
questions.explanation: Ionic naming: cation name + polyatomic ion name. Aluminum + sulfate = aluminum sulfate. No Roman numeral needed (Al is always +3). No prefixes (this is ionic, not covalent). The polyatomic ion keeps its name intact.

questions.question: NHв‚„вЃє (ammonium) is unusual because it's a positive polyatomic ion. In NHв‚„Cl, which is the cation and which is the anion?

options.A: N is the cation, Hв‚„Cl is the anion
options.B: NHв‚„вЃє is the cation, ClвЃ» is the anion
options.C: Cl is the cation, NHв‚„ is the anion
options.D: There are no ions вЂ” this is covalent

questions.explanation: NHв‚„вЃє (ammonium) is the cation вЂ” a positive polyatomic ion acting like a metal. ClвЃ» (chloride) is the anion. The compound is ionic: ammonium chloride. NHв‚„вЃє is treated as a single unit, just like NaвЃє would be.

parts.purpose: Consolidate naming/formula skills, bridge to chemical_reactions

blocks.title: The Complete Naming Algorithm
blocks.body: You now have a complete system for naming any compound and writing any formula.

items.label: Identify: Ionic or Covalent?
items.body: Contains a metal (or NHв‚„вЃє)? в†’ Ionic. All nonmetals? в†’ Covalent.

items.label: If Ionic: Metal name + nonmetal-ide (or polyatomic name)
items.body: Add Roman numeral for transition metals. Write formula using criss-cross. Parentheses for multiple polyatomic ions.

items.label: If Covalent: Prefix + first element + prefix + second element-ide
items.body: Use Greek prefixes (mono, di, tri...). Drop mono- from first element. Write formula using the prefixes as subscripts.

blocks.body: This naming system is the LANGUAGE of chemistry. Every equation you write, every reaction you describe, every lab report you complete uses these names and formulas. Mastering this now makes everything else easier.

You can now name compounds and write formulas. The next step: chemical reactions вЂ” equations that describe what happens when bonds break and new bonds form. You'll learn to read equations like sentences, balance them like math, and classify reactions by type. Chemical reactions are where static formulas come alive.

blocks.title: Final Speed Round: Name в†” Formula

items.label: Feв‚‚(SOв‚„)в‚ѓ в†’ ?
items.hints: Ionic with polyatomic ion. Iron(III) sulfate (Fe is +3: 3 sulfates at в€’2 each = в€’6, so 2 Fe = +6 в†’ each Fe = +3).

blocks.body: If you can do these four examples, you can name and write the formula for any compound in introductory chemistry. The system is complete.
Formulas describe compounds at rest. Chemical reactions describe compounds in motion вЂ” transforming into new substances. That's next.

blocks.title: You Now Speak Chemistry
blocks.body: If someone says 'dinitrogen monoxide,' you know it's Nв‚‚O (laughing gas). If someone writes Caв‚ѓ(POв‚„)в‚‚, you know it's calcium phosphate (your bones). You're bilingual: English and Chemistry.

DHMO (dihydrogen monoxide) is a famous internet hoax where people petition to 'ban' this 'dangerous chemical' вЂ” it's just water (Hв‚‚O). The covalent naming system makes everything sound scary. Sodium chloride sounds like a chemical weapon. It's table salt. Never trust a name without checking the formula.

We've learned the vocabulary (element names), the grammar (formulas and naming), and the structure (bonding). Now it's time to write full SENTENCES: chemical equations. When bonds break and reform, atoms rearrange, and entirely new substances appear. That's a chemical reaction. And learning to write and balance reaction equations is where chemistry gets truly powerful.

questions.question: An unknown compound has the formula Crв‚‚Oв‚ѓ. Chromium is a transition metal. What is the correct name?

options.B: Chromium(III) oxide вЂ” each Cr is +3 (total +6 balances 3 OВІвЃ» at в€’6)

questions.explanation: Ionic compound (metal + nonmetal). Cr is a transition metal в†’ needs Roman numeral. 3 Г— OВІвЃ» = в€’6. For 2 Cr to balance: 2x = +6, x = +3. Name: chromium(III) oxide. Option C uses covalent prefixes (wrong for ionic). Option D has wrong charge.
